title: 1 High-Flying Stock for Long-Term Investors and 2 We Question
content:
Expensive stocks typically earn their valuations through superior growth rates that other companies simply can’t match. The flip side though is that these lofty expectations make them particularly susceptible to drawdowns when market sentiment shifts.

Finding the right balance between price and quality can challenge even the most skilled investors. Luckily for you, we started StockStory to help you identify the real opportunities. That said, here is one high-flying stock to hold for the long term and two where the price is not right.

Two High-Flying Stocks to Sell:

Vishay Precision (VPG)

Forward P/E Ratio: 56.7x

Emerging from Vishay Intertechnology in 2010, Vishay Precision (NYSE:VPG) operates as a global provider of precision measurement and sensing technologies.

Why Is VPG Risky?

Sales tumbled by 7% annually over the last two years, showing market trends are working against its favor during this cycle Performance over the past five years shows its incremental sales were much less profitable, as its earnings per share fell by 16.2% annually Diminishing returns on capital from an already low starting point show that neither management’s prior nor current bets are going as planned

Vishay Precision is trading at $46.39 per share, or 56.7x forward P/E. To fully understand why you should be careful with VPG, check out our full research report (it’s free).

Surgery Partners (SGRY)

Forward P/E Ratio: 61.5x

With more than 180 locations across 33 states serving as alternatives to traditional hospital settings, Surgery Partners (NASDAQ:SGRY) operates a national network of outpatient surgical facilities including ambulatory surgery centers and short-stay surgical hospitals.

Why Do We Think Twice About SGRY?

Underwhelming unit sales over the past two years suggest it might have to lower prices to accelerate growth Estimated sales growth of 2.9% for the next 12 months implies demand will slow from its two-year trend High net-debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 7× could force the company to raise capital at unfavorable terms if market conditions deteriorate

At $12.66 per share, Surgery Partners trades at 61.5x forward P/E. Check out our free in-depth research report to learn more about why SGRY doesn’t pass our bar.

One High-Flying Stock to Watch:

Helmerich & Payne (HP)

Forward P/E Ratio: 66x

Operating the largest fleet of super-spec rigs in North America with technology that can drill horizontal wells over two miles long, Helmerich & Payne (NYSE:HP) provides drilling rigs and crews to oil and gas companies that need wells drilled to extract hydrocarbons from underground.

Story Continues

Why Should HP Be on Your Watchlist?

Annual revenue growth of 25.4% over the last five years was superb and indicates its market share increased during this cycle Revenue base of $4.09 billion gives it economies of scale and some negotiating power with suppliers EBITDA profits and efficiency rose over the last five years as it benefited from some fixed cost leverage

Helmerich & Payne’s stock price of $34.93 implies a valuation ratio of 66x forward P/E. Is now the right time to buy? Find out in our full research report, it’s free.

High-Quality Stocks for All Market Conditions

title: Should Helmerich & Payne’s Utica Square Sale And Debt Cut Require Action From HP Investors?
content:
Helmerich & Payne has completed the sale of long-held Tulsa retail center Utica Square to funds managed by Northwood Investors, using the after-tax proceeds to largely retire its remaining term loan balance as part of its debt reduction plan. This divestiture marks a clear move to streamline the post–KCA Deutag portfolio around core drilling solutions while improving balance sheet flexibility. Next, we’ll explore how using Utica Square sale proceeds to cut term debt could reshape Helmerich & Payne’s investment narrative.

Find 59 companies with promising cash flow potential yet trading below their fair value.

Helmerich & Payne Investment Narrative Recap

To own Helmerich & Payne, you need to believe that high spec drilling and digital tools can stay in demand while the company steadily improves its balance sheet after the KCA Deutag deal. The Utica Square sale helps here by quickly cutting term debt, but it does not change the core near term catalyst around utilization and pricing for super spec rigs, nor the key risk of potential overcapacity if North American drilling activity weakens further.

The Utica Square divestiture fits closely with earlier updates around the KCA Deutag acquisition, where management outlined portfolio optimization and cost synergies of US$50 million to US$75 million. Those synergies, alongside ongoing digitalization and automation, are central to the thesis that H&P can lift margins even if overall rig counts stay under pressure. This new, lower leverage profile could give the company more room to support those efforts if market conditions become more challenging.

Yet beneath the balance sheet progress, investors should be aware of how regional overcapacity and contract pressure could interact with H&P’s heavy capital needs and relatively high fixed cost base...

Read the full narrative on Helmerich & Payne (it's free!)

Helmerich & Payne's narrative projects $3.9 billion revenue and $276.0 million earnings by 2028. This requires 4.3% yearly revenue growth and a $309.0 million earnings increase from -$33.0 million today.

Uncover how Helmerich & Payne's forecasts yield a $30.27 fair value, a 13% downside to its current price.

Exploring Other PerspectivesHP 1-Year Stock Price Chart

Some of the lowest estimate analysts are far more cautious, even before this sale, projecting only about US$3.6 billion of revenue and US$232 million of earnings by 2028, so if you are weighing this debt reduction against concerns about long term cost pressures and demand for drilling, it is worth seeing how much these more pessimistic views might shift after the Utica Square news.
